questions.q: A pod has two init containers. The first succeeds but the second fails. What happens?
options: The main container starts using only the first init container's result
The pod is marked Failed permanently
The pod restarts and runs both init containers again from the beginning
The second init container is skipped and the main container starts
questions.explanation: If any init container fails, the entire pod is restarted (according to restartPolicy). All init containers run again from the beginning — there is no partial state preserved between restarts.

questions.q: Init containers run in parallel with each other.
questions.explanation: False. Init containers run sequentially — one at a time, in the order they are defined. Each must exit successfully before the next one starts. Only the main containers start in parallel (after all init containers complete).

questions.q: Which pod status indicates that the first of two init containers is currently running?
questions.explanation: Init:0/2 means 0 out of 2 init containers have completed (the first is currently running). Init:1/2 would mean the first completed and the second is running. PodInitializing appears after all init containers finish but before main containers start.

questions.q: Which is a valid use case for an init container?
options: Serving HTTP traffic while the main container warms up
Waiting for a dependency service to become available before starting the main app
questions.explanation: Init containers are ideal for dependency checks (wait for DB, wait for API), pre-populating shared volumes, running DB migrations, or any setup that must complete before the main app starts.
